coredata.dc:title: Response surface methodology optimization for the synthesis of N, S-codoped carbon dots and its application for tetracyclines detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wei
Li, Shaoqing
Yin, Pengyuan
Li, Jiaxiong
Tang, Yi
Yang, Min
coredata.dc:description: In this study, using a convenient one-pot microwave-assisted method, we rapidly fabricated a N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots (NSCDs) through using citric acid (CA) and d -penicillamine (DPA) for the detection of tetracyclines (TC). To rapidly and efficiently optimize the various synthesis parameters and significantly decrease the number of experimental runs, the effect of the synthesis factors on quantum yield (QY) by NSCDs was implemented through means of response surface methodology (RSM). The as-synthesized NSCDs presented superior photoluminescence stability with a strong quantum yield (QY) of 91.55% under optimal conditions, which was consistent with the predicted value using RSM. The fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs could be quenched effectively after adding TC by the inner filter effect (IEE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Thus, the determination of TC by using NSCDs as a facile fluorescent probe was constructed in the range of 0.2–70 μM with a limit detection of 0.072 μM. Moreover, this detection approach has been utilized to detect TC in actual samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave-assistance
Response surface methodology
Fluorescent probe
Tetracyclines

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Challenges in Environmental Science & Engineering - Persistent and Emerging Pollutants: Identification to Treatment and Remediation for Sustainable Environments Edited by Veeriah Jegatheesan, Tsair-Fuh Lin, Shu Li, and Eldon R. Rene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions by a Box-Behnken design (BBD)
The sensing mechanism for detecting TC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Three-dimensional (3D) response surface plots: the effects of microwave power (X1) and irradiation time (X2) (a), microwave power (X1) and the molar ratio of DPA/CA (X3) (b), irradiation time (X2) and the molar ratio of DPA/CA (X3) (c) variables on the QY response, two-dimensional (2D) contour line graph (d–f).
TEM and high resolution (HR) TEM images (inset) of NSCDs (a). The particle size distribution of NSCDs as determined by DLS (b). FT-IR spectrum of the NSCDs (c). XPS survey spectrum of the NSCDs (d).
UV–vis and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of NSCDs (a). Fluorescence emission spectra of NSCDs under the various excitation wavelengths (b).

The fluorescence spectra of NSCDs at the different TC concentrations (0–70 μM) (a). A linear calibration plot between F0/F and the concentration of TC (0.2–70 μM) (b). The F0/F of NSCDs in response to the various ions and small molecules at the concentration of 50 μM (blue bars), NSCDs towards different ions and small molecules (100 μM) upon addition of 50 μM TC (cyan bars) (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

UV–vis spectrum of TC and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of NSCDs (a). Fluorescence lifetime of NSCDs and NSCDs +50 μM TC (b). The electronic bandgap was calculated by the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NSCDs (3 mg/L) (c). Schematic illustration of electron transfer mechanism of NSCDs and TC (d).
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of NSCDs and TC detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Significant when “Prob. > F″ is less than 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Applications of NSCDs in the real samples for TC determination.

head.ce:title: Response surface methodology optimization for the synthesis of N, S-codoped carbon dots and its application for tetracyclines detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mathematics and Computer Science, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming, 650500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mathematics and Computer Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mathematics and Computer Science, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, PR. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions, Education Department of Yunnan, School of Chemistry and Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming, 650500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions, Education Department of Yunnan, School of Chemistry and Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, PR. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Wang and Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, using a convenient one-pot microwave-assisted method, we rapidly fabricated a N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots (NSCDs) through using citric acid (CA) and d -penicillamine (DPA) for the detection of tetracyclines (TC). To rapidly and efficiently optimize the various synthesis parameters and significantly decrease the number of experimental runs, the effect of the synthesis factors on quantum yield (QY) by NSCDs was implemented through means of response surface methodology (RSM). The as-synthesized NSCDs presented superior photoluminescence stability with a strong quantum yield (QY) of 91.55% under optimal conditions, which was consistent with the predicted value using RSM. The fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs could be quenched effectively after adding TC by the inner filter effect (IEE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Thus, the determination of TC by using NSCDs as a facile fluorescent probe was constructed in the range of 0.2–70 μM with a limit detection of 0.072 μM. Moreover, this detection approach has been utilized to detect TC in actual samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials, owing to their outstanding merits, have been widely used in chemistry and biology. Among them, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with an ultra-small size (<10 nm) contain a carbon skeleton core with sp2/sp3 and abundant oxygen-containing groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl and aldehyde groups (Zhang and Yu, 2016). Thus, it possesses the advantages of high water solubility and an easily chemically modified surface (Zheng et al., 2015). In addition, compared with conventional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have been widely employed for sensing (Sun et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020), bioimaging (Yang et al., 2018), photocatalysis (Hui et al., 2022), optical devices (Dai et al., 2022) etc. as they have exhibited outstanding photostability, low cost and extraordinary biocompatibility. Some of synthesis strategies used for CDs have been constructed, including ligand-induced etching (Liu et al., 2020), pyrolysis (Li et al., 2019), hydrothermal/solvent methods (Ding et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2020) and microwave-assisted synthesis (Yang et al., 2018). Especially microwave-assisted techniques have received great attention in recent years because they can provide simple experimental conditions, shorter reaction times, and uniform heating (Li et al., 2022a,b). In general, the precursors of CDs primarily originate from the bulk graphitized carbon materials, biomass and a variety of small organic molecule from inexpensive reagents (Ding et al., 2020; Anusuya et al., 2021). Among the prepared CDs, organic small-molecule carbonization not only showcased distinctive advantages, such as simple operation, high quantum yields, and tunable emission colors (Shen et al., 2018), but also can yield various rich functional groups, which can be efficiently used as a potential candidate for sensing (Li et al., 2017).

Although the synthesized CDs have been recognized as the efficient fluorescent probes, some of them still are still associated with some drawbacks such as low quantum yield and poor stability (Zheng et al., 2011). Thus, a series of strategies have been analyzed to modulate their luminescence properties, such as configurations of the surface functional groups, doping atoms and surface oxidation (Ding et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2020; Krishnaiah et al., 2022). In particular, heteroatom doping can serve as a powerful strategy that can effectively modulate the band gap and electron density to substantially improve the chemical activity and quantum yield (Shi et al., 2017). For instance, Li and his colleagues reported a N, S co-doped CDs with a strong fluorescence (QY 64%) by using a microwave heating approach which employed ammonium citrate and l-cysteine as raw materials (Li et al., 2018). By using a similar method, Zhang and co-workers designed green fluorescence-emitting N, S CDs by glutathione (GSH) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as precursors (Zhang et al., 2022). Due to the doping element, CDs have been endowed with rich functional groups, high stability, high quantum yields (63%) and low toxicity. Based on these properties, intracellular detection of Fe3+ and thiols was achieved by “ON-OFF-ON” mechanism.

Tetracyclines (TCs), a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics, have been widely employed both for preventing and treating bacterial infections in animals or as food additives to aid the growth of livestock due to their significant inhibitory effects on pathogenic microorganisms and low cost (Feng et al., 2015). However, excessive use of TC can lead to extensive accumulation in animals which can then migrate to the top of the food chain, thereby augmenting the potential risks to the human health, including liver damage, drug resistance, and allergic symptoms and etc (Chopra and Roberts, 2001; Wang et al., 2022). Till date, diverse strategies such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Wang et al., 2008), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Ben et al., 2008), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Meyer et al., 2000), and chemiluminescence (Townshend et al., 2005), have been developed for the determination of TC. However, all these methods possess the practical disadvantages such as complex pre-treatment, time-consuming and expensive equipment configuration and expertise (J. Wang et al., 2021), which are unsuitable for optimal application in analysis. In this regard, the fluorescence analysis method can be more suitable for the quantitative detection of TC due to its ease of operation, rapid response, good selectivity, and lower costs (Guo et al., 2021).

For the optimization of the synthesis conditions, single-factor experiments are tedious as well as challenging, thus ignoring the interaction between multiple factors, resulting in low accuracy, efficiency and time-consuming process (Nemati et al., 2018). Response surface methodology (RSM), acting as a powerful mathematical and statistical mean, could be employed to predict the optimum and assess the influences of specific parameters and their possible interactive responses (Chen et al., 2018; Vyavahare et al., 2018). It can also allow to minimize the number of trial-and-error runs as well as expenses, and maintain sufficient precision for the process optimization (Medinger et al., 2021; Isaac et al., 2020). The Box-Behnken design (BBD) approach is the most commonly chosen under RSM design to significantly improve accuracy of the predicted model and frequently determine for the various optimal conditions (Jaafari and Yaghmaeian, 2019). Here, a blue-emitting N, S codoped CDs was fabricated from CA and DPA by microwave-assisted strategy. The BBD model was used to study and optimize the effects of the different independent parameters like microwave power, irradiation time and the molar ratio of DPA/CA. The structure and properties of NSCDs were further characterized by TEM, XPS, FT-IR and other techniques. As the NSCDs can be efficiently quenched via TC, a selective fluorescent probe based on TC quantitative analysis was also designed. Furthermore, the practicality of the proposed probe was confirmed by monitoring spiked tap water, milk and honey samples. (Scheme 1 )

All the chemicals were AR grade. Citric acid (CA), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, KCl, KNO3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), d -Penicillamine (DPA), NaCl and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were purchased from Adamas (Shanghai, China). CaCl2, ZnCl2, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, histidine (His), l -arginine (L-Arg), glutathione (GSH), l -cysteine (L-Cys), l -glutamic acid (L-Glu), l -phenylalanine (L-Phe), ascorbic acid (AA), penicillin G sodium (PenG), chloramphenicol (CAT), azithromycin (ADR), gentamicin (GM) and enrofloxacin (ENR) were supplied by Macklin (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water produced by a Purifier DZG-303A ultrapure water system (Purifier, China) was used throughout all the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were measured using on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) at a slit width of 5 nm. The morphology of NSCDs was analyzed by JEM-2100 plus TEM analyzer (JEOL, Japan). The size distribution was determined Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern, UK). The functional groups of NSCDs were measured with a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA). XPS spectra of NSCDs were recorded using a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha + spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2100 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The decay curves were performed by a FLS1000 lifetime spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). The cyclic voltammogram curve of NSCDs was acquired on a CH 1600E electrochemical workstation (Huachen, China).

N, S-codoped carbon dots from CA and DPA were synthesized through an effectively modified microwave-assisted method (Xuan et al., 2017). The various factors such as microwave power, irradiation time, pH, solvent and the molar ratio of DPA/CA can exhibit substantial influence on the synthesis of NSCDs. In consideration of simple and green manufacturing, distilled water was selected as the solvent and no adjustment in the pH in the solution was made during the whole experiment. Accordingly, a standard Box-Behnken design approach was applied to optimize the synthesis of NSCDs. As described in Table S1, three independent variables, microwave power (X1), irradiation time (X2), and the molar ratio of DPA/CA (X3) were selected as the variables. Fluorescence quantum yield (Y) was examined as a dependent response. The experimental design was implemented by Design Expert version 10. Based on the preliminary experiments, all the variables were fixed at 3 levels (−1, 0, 1) with X1 (540, 720, 900 W), X2 (2, 5, 8 min), and X3 (0.2, 0.6, 1). The optimization experiment of the three independent variables using the quadratic equation model was expressed as follows in Eq. (1):
(1)
Y =
β 0
+
∑
i = 1
k
β i
X i
+
∑
i = 1
k
β
i i
X i 2
+
∑
i = 1
k
∑
j > 1
k
β
i j
X i
X j

Here, Y represents the predicted response of quantum yield (QY). Xi and Xj stand for the synthesis variables. β0 denotes the intercept. βi, βii and βij are implied to be linear, quadratic, and the interaction effect coefficients, respectively.

Briefly, 1.0507 g CA and the desired amount of DPA (see Table S2) were mixed with 10 mL ultrapure water. The solution was placed inside a commercial microwave oven (Galanz, C5) at the different microwave power for the varying durations (Table S2). After the reaction was over, the crude NSCDs was lowered to the room temperature and then dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. After using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane filter for the filtration, the liquid was transferred into a dialysis bag of 500–1000 Da for purification. Finally, solid NSCDs were obtained through a vacuum freeze dryer.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of NSCDs was established via a relative method (Tang et al., 2021a). Quinine sulfate was selected as the reference (QY = 54%, 0.1 M H2SO4). The absorbance of the reference solution and the NSCDs solution were controlled below 0.05 to minimize the self-absorption effect. The QY of NSCDs was determined via Eq. (2):
(2)
φ x
=
φ s
×
(
k x
k s
)
×
(
η x
η s
)
2

Here, φx and φs represent QY of NSCDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; kx and ks stand for the integral area of the fluorescence spectra NSCDs and quinine sulfate (λex = 350 nm), respectively; ηx and ηs are the refractive index of the NSCDs and quinine sulfate (ηx/ηs is about equal to 1), respectively.

90 μL of NSCDs solution (100 mg/L) and the various concentrations of then standard TC solution were mixed into a cuvette. The mixture was attenuated to 3 mL with PB buffer solutions (20 mM, pH = 6). The fluorescence spectra with the slit width of 5 nm were recorded after incubation at the room temperature for 2 min. For evaluating the selectivity of NSCDs, common inorganic ions, amino acids and antibiotics (50 μM K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, His, L-Arg, GSH, L-Lcy, L-Glu, L-Phe, AA, PenG, CAT, ADR, GM and ENR) were respectively mixed with probe system for the fluorescence measurement. In addition, to further verify the anti-interfering ability, 50 μM TC and 100 μM of the above interfering substances were separately added to the system.

Considering the practicality of TC, the three actual samples were estimated with the N, S-CDs fluorescent probe. The tap water was directly obtained. Milk (in 100 g of milk, 3 g protein, 3.5 g fat, 4.8 g carbohydrate) and the honey samples from the local supermarket were carried out based on the previous reported method (Xu et al., 2021). Firstly, 0.1 mL trichloroacetic acid solution (300 g/L) was mixed with 10 mL milk sample. After the sonication using an ultrasonic cleaner for 20 min, the collected mixture was purified using a high-speed centrifugation (Hermle, Z446) under 10,000 r/min for 10 min to remove both the proteins and lipids. Thereafter, the obtained liquid was filtered with 0.22 μM microporous membrane filter. Honey sample was diluted 20 times using ultrapure water and then filtered through the 0.22 μm microporous membrane filter. Finally, several concentrations of TC (0, 10 and 20 μM) were added to actual samples and determined via the presented approach in the step 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions by a Box-Behnken design (BBD)
ce:section.ce:para:
Organic small-molecule carbonization through a convenient microwave-assisted technique have been widely employed to fabricate CDs (Li et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2018). Citric acid, as an inexpensive and easily available raw material, has commonly served as an ideal carbon precursor. Carbon dots have been fabricated to produce highly luminescence particles owing to DPA with the rich nitrogen and sulfur atoms doping into the CDs. In this study, BBD was used to optimize the NSCDs synthesis, containing microwave power (X1), irradiation time (X2), and the molar ratio of DPA/CA (X3). Table S2 shows the synthesis conditions and results of 15 runs using the BBD. In addition, to further analyze and compare the experimental data, a quadratic polynomial regression equation Eq. (1) was used to the response value (QY) as a function of the three design factors by Eq. (3).
(3)
Y
( Predicted % Q Y
o f
N ,
S − C D s )
=
90.06
+
(
3.55 ∗
X 1
)
+
(
9.28 ∗
X 2
)
+
(
2.09 ∗
X 3
)
−
(
5.48 ∗
X 1
∗
X 2
)
−
(
0.18 ∗
X 1
∗
X 3
)
−
(
1.19 ∗
X 2
∗
X 3
)
−
(
13.61 ∗
X 1 2
)
−
(
9.38 ∗
X 2 2
)
−
(
6.34 ∗
X 3 2
)

According to the above equations for Y, there were high coefficients in the models for the X2 term (9.28), the quadratic term of X1 (−13.61) and X2 (−9.38), thereby indicating that the microwave power and irradiation time are the main factors for obtaining high QY. They play a pivotal role in the formation of NSCDs for controlling the hydrolysis and carbonization processes (Dela Cruz et al., 2019). An analysis of variance (ANOVA) indicated that the P-value of the proposed model was lower than 0.0002, indicating that the model was highly significant for QY (Table 1 ). Among them, the P-values of X1, X2, X3, X1X2, X1 2, X2 2 and X3 2 were found to be less than 0.05, thereby showing that they were significant. The lack of fit values displayed a significance if the model was not fit well (Myers et al., 2016). When the lack of fit P-value was 0.2056 (>0.05), F-value (4.02) manifested that the model was no-significant, and the model correlation between the variables and the QY responses of NSCDs was significant (Nemati et al., 2018). Furthermore, As shown in Table 1, the model with a value of coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9899), adjusted R2 value (Adj-R2 = 0.9718) and the predicted R2 (Pred. R2 = 0.8586) were close to 1. It suggested that a good relationship between experimental data and empirical model can be effectively applied to this model for predicting optimal response. A high precision value of 25.239 (more than 4 critical value) was obtained, which indicated optimal signals and models to guide the design space (Noordin et al., 2004).

The reliability and applicability of the quadratic model within the experimental range was also analyzed in detail (Fig. S1). As observed in, Fig. S1a the actual and predicted data was found to be close to the straight line approximately, thereby suggesting that the proposed model can completely estimate the QY value of the experiment. As can be observed from Fig. S1a, the residuals versus predicted QY plot revealed the normal distribution of the data, which confirmed that the model for the synthesis of NSCDs using BBD was valid.

Moreover, to further discuss and analyze the interaction influence of two factors on the QY response and the change of the curved surface, 3D and 2D graphs were presented in Fig. 1 . Among them, Fig. 1a and d showed the response surface and contour plots of the QY response as a function of X1 and X2. It was observed that with an increase of the microwave power, QY response gradually increased first and then decreased significantly. Similarly, QY increased, and decreased after 7 min with the increase of irradiation time. It has been established that microwave power and irradiation time can provide the temperature on the reaction system. In addition, the precursor molecules can undergo polymerization and condensation after continuous heating, and finally carbonization to obtain more CDs with internal sp2 domains of graphitic-like structure (Dong et al., 2012; Medeiros et al., 2019). However, the prolonged exposure to the microwave caused excessive dehydration and carbonization during the formation of NSCDs, leading to the quenching of the nonradiative inactivation pathway. The potential influence of microwave power and the molar ratio of DPA/CA on QY response was displayed in Fig. 1b and e. The QY response was found to be significantly stronger as the molar ratio increased in the range of 0.2–0.7 and a further increase led to a marked decrease of QY. On the one hand, the introduction of DPA can contribute more functional groups (-COOH, –NH2) and induce a higher degree of the graphitization in the reaction (S. Wang et al., 2021). On the other hand, because of the synergistic coupling impact between the doped N and S atoms, the charge distribution can be tuned to reduce the nonradiative recombination and band gap, thereby enhancing the luminescent properties of CDs. Unfortunately, excessive DPA can obstruct the conjugation with CA to generate CDs. This is primarily due to the fact that a certain amount of CA contains three carboxyl groups and one hydroxyl group, which are unable to provide sufficient reaction sites for excess DPA (Chahal et al., 2020). In addition, Fig. 1c and f displayed that QY increased as irradiation time and the molar ratio of DPA/CA increased, whereas increasing carbonization time and the molar ratio, the QY of NSCDs slightly decreased.

Finally, the prediction of the synthesis conditions and experimental verification were also examined. The QY response quadratic polynomial regression model for the synthesis of NSCDs was optimized and obtained as follows: 726 W, 6.4 min and 0.65 for microwave power, irradiation time and the molar ratio of DPA/CA, respectively. The predicted value of QY response was found to be 92.46%. To facilitate the experimental operation, the conditions were adjusted as follows: 720 W, 6 min and 0.65 for three distinct variables, respectively. The actual value of QY response is 91.55 ± 2.7%, and the error was only 0.98% compared with the predicted value, thereby indicating that the model is suitable, effective and feasible.

Firstly, both the morphology and size of the NSCDs were analyzed via TEM (Fig. 2 a). The NSCDs appeared quasi-spherical and uniformly distributed. HRTEM image showed a typical spacing of 0.21 nm, as observed by the inset of Fig. 2a, representing the (100) plane of graphite (Li et al., 2020). In addition, the particle exhibited an average size of about 2.19 nm in the range of 1.5–3.5 nm by DLS (Fig. 2b). In the FT-IR spectrum of NSCDs (Fig. 2c), a broad peak at near 3400 cm−1 was observed, owing to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. The peaks located at 3100, 1520 and 1077 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C–H, CON–H and C–S, respectively (Hao et al., 2021). The presence of the stretching vibrations of CO, C–N and C–O–C was confirmed by the obvious peaks at 1718 1398 and 1203 cm−1 respectively (Tang et al., 2021). These results implied that the –OH, –COOH and –NH2 groups existed in the synthesized NSCDs, and could endow an excellent hydrophilic property and potential detection capability (Jiang et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2019). To further identify the various functional groups and chemical composition, XPS was used to study the obtained NSCDs. In the full survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d), four peaks centered at 285.02, 400.19, 532.12 and 163.3 eV, attributing to C 1s, N1s, O 1s and S 2p, respectively. Thereafter, the HRXPS spectrum of C1s was decomposed into four distinct binding energies (Fig S2a), which were assigned C–C/CC (284.69 eV), C–O/C–O–C (286.09 eV), CN (288.18 eV), and CO (288.93 eV) (Han et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2021). The N 1S spectrum depicted three different nitrogen components at 399.86, 400.44, and 401.16 eV, which were affiliated to C–N–C, N-(C)3 and N–H bonds, respectively, as shown in Fig. S2b (Hu et al., 2019). Moreover, two peaks located at 531.79 and 533.07 eV in the O 1S curve (Fig. S2c) were attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively (Chu and Li, 2006; Ding et al., 2016). Furthermore, the two distinct peaks at 163.36 and 164.47 eV could also be seen in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. S2d), corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 in the C–S bond, respectively (Shi et al., 2017).

The photoluminescence properties of the NSCDs aqueous solution were also explored by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Two distinct absorption peaks at around 240 and 350 nm were observed in Fig. 3 a, attributing to the π-π* transition of CC bond and the n-π* transition of the surface state containing CO and CN bonds, respectively (Xu et al., 2018; Bai et al., 2019). In addition, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of NSCDs overlapped with the UV–vis absorption bands at 350 nm, which suggested that the fluorescence emission was primarily associated with n-π* transition (Li et al., 2020). Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of NSCDs at 425 nm exhibited maximum intensity upon excitation at 350 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b. The emission peak shown a significant excitation-independent property when excited from 290 to 390 nm, which was ascribed to the similar diameters and surface state of NSCDs (Li et al., 2022a,b).

The stability, such as pH, saline solution, UV light irradiation and storage time, act as important properties for sensing applications. First, NSCDs exhibited a relatively weakened fluorescence in strongly acid (pH < 3.0) and strong base solutions (pH > 11) (Fig. S3a). This stems primarily from the electronic transition of the luminescent center on the surface of NSCDs was destroyed by protonation and deprotonation, resulting in fluorescence quenched (Pan et al., 2010; Tang et al., 2021). However, the fluorescence retained stable in the pH range of 5–11, which was beneficial to practical applications in the biological circumstances. Subsequently, as illustrated in Fig. S3b, the salt solution stability of NSCDs was evaluated at the various NaCl concentrations (0–2.0 M). The fluorescence intensity was found to be almost unaffected even at a 2.0 M high concentration, confirming the excellent salt stability of NSCDs. Moreover, as represented in Fig. S3c, under continuous irradiation of UV light within 60 min, the fluorescence of NSCDs was observed to be decreased by only less than 7%, which demonstrated a good resistance to photobleaching. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs did not fluctuate significantly after storage for 30 days (Fig. S3d), thus indicating that NSCDs can sustain stability for a long period of time. Overall, under these conditions, the NSCDs aqueous solutions displayed the excellent luminescence stability.

Based on their superb merits in terms of the photoluminescence stability, TC was detected using NSCDs. It was found that with increasing TC concentration, the fluorescence gradually was quenched (Fig. 4 a), displaying a typical concentration-dependent behavior. A virtuous linear relationship between the fluorescence signal (F0/F) and TC concentration within a wide range of 0.2–70 μM has been shown in Fig. 4b. The following Eq. (4) was obtained to quantitatively detect TC:
(4)
F 0
/ F
=
0.0277
[ T C ]
+
0.9924
(
R 2
=
0.995 )
where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs and NSCDs with TC, respectively. [TC] represents the concentration of TC. A low limit of detection (LOD) was obtained as 0.072 μM based on 3σ/k (σ stands for the standard deviation of the blank samples, and k represents the slope of the linear range). In addition, the influence of the incubation time for the determination of TC was analyzed (Fig. S4). It was noted that in the presence of 50 μM TC, the maximum and stable quenched fluorescence was achieved after only 2 min interaction, indicating that it can achieve a rapid determination for TC. Moreover, upon comparison of other fluorescent nanomaterials for detection of TC in the previously reported literature as shown in Table S3, the proposed NSCDs exhibited a fast response, wider linear range and relatively lower LOD, which could be effectively employed for the determination in actual samples.

To verify the potential specificity of the fluorescence probe for TC, common inorganic ions and small molecules (50 μM) was investigated, including K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, His, L-Arg, GSH, L-Lcy, L-Glu, L-Phe, AA, PenG, CAT, ADR, GM and ENR. As indicated in Fig. 4c, only the addition of TC caused a substantial fluorescence quenching, whereas other ions and small molecules did not have a significant effect. In addition, when other ions and small molecules (100 μM) coexisted with TC, TC can still effectively quench the fluorescence and did not display any obvious effects (Fig. 4c). The results suggested that NSCDs for detecting TC not only possessed high selectivity but also had excellent anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism for detecting TC
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the sensing mechanism, the optical properties of TC and NSCDs were examined (Fig. 5 a). In the UV–vis spectrum of TC, two peaks centered at 275 nm and 356 nm were observed, respectively (Zhang et al., 2021). Among them, the peak at 356 nm was noted to overlap well with the excitation spectrum of NSCDs, suggesting that the dramatic fluorescence quenching was caused by IFE (Zhao et al., 2018). Thereafter, the fluorescence quenching behavior was examined via steady-state/lifetime spectrofluorometer (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence lifetime of NSCDs was 9.45 ns. Noticeably, it remained almost unchanged after addition of 50 mM TC (9.35 ns), which demonstrated that the quenching mechanism could undergo a static quenching because no energy transfer process occurred between NSCDs and TC (Guo et al., 2021).

In addition, to verify the presence or absence the fluorescence quenching by photoinduced electron transfer (PET), both the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of NSCDs were calculated by using the empirical Eqs. (5) and (6) (Liang et al., 2006):
(5)
E LUMO
=
− e
(
E
r e d
+ 4.4 )
(6)
E HOMO
=
− e
(
E
o x
+ 4.4 )
where Ered and Eox are the reduction and oxidation potential of NSCDs, respectively. First, the cyclic voltammetry of NSCDs was measured with an Ag/AgCl (saturated in 3.0 M of KCl) as reference electrode in PB buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 6). The Ered of NSCDs was found to be −0.64 eV (Fig. S5). According to Eq. (5), the ELUMO of NSCDs was calculated to be −3.76 eV. However, because of the irreversibility of the oxidation process, the EHOMO could not be calculated by Eq. (6) (Tang et al., 2021). Thus, to determine the EHOMO, the electronic bandgap (Eg) of NSCDs was obtained to be 5.32 eV based on the relation of (αhν)2 against the photon energy in absorption spectrum (Fig. 5c). The EHOMO of NSCDs was estimated to be −9.08 eV by Eq. (7) (Liang et al., 2006; Amin et al., 2018).
(7)
E HOMO
=
E
L U M O
−
E g

Based on reported literature (Yan et al., 2019), the EHOMO and ELUMO of TC were found to be −6.2 eV and −3.87 eV, respectively. Since the ELUMO of TC was lower than that of NSCDs, the excited state electrons of NSCDs from the LUMO could be directly transferred to the LUMO of TC (Fig. 5d). Therefore, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) could occur between NSCDs and TC. Thus, the sensing mechanism for detecting TC was believed to be mainly dominated by IFE and PET.

The practical application of NSCDs for TC detection was further validated in the real samples, including the tap water, milk and honey. The different concentrations of TC were added into tap water, milk and honey as the standard solutions (0, 10 and 20 μM). The measured recoveries of TC in these samples were all between 96.5 and 108.0% with RSD less than 5% (Table 2 ). The above results indicated that the method had excellent potential for the quantitative analysis of TC in the real samples.

In this article, a facile, rapid and environment friendly route to construct N, S codoped carbon dots (NSCDs) was utilized to develop a novel fluorescent probe for detecting TC. The optimization of the synthesis conditions was completed by utilizing BBD approach as a potential type of RSM. The results revealed that the developed model and experimental procedure for the synthesized NSCDs exhibited high degree of feasibility. Based on the optimized conditions, NSCDs with an acceptable quantum yield was also obtained. The as-prepared NSCDs showcased an outstanding fluorescence stability under the various conditions with a size of around 2.19 nm. Moreover, on the basis of the fluorescence quenched by TC, a simple, quick and sensitive approach was established for the determination of TC. The developed method also demonstrated LOD of 0.072 μM, with the detection of TC range from 0.2 to 70 μM. Besides, NSCDs fluorescent probe was used to measure TC in actual samples, and satisfactory results (96.5–108.0%) were achieved in all the samples. This work provided a novel approach to construct a sensing platform based on the tetracycline detection.

Wei Wang: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shaoqing Li: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Pengyuan Yin: Data curation, Investigation. Jiaxiong Li: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yi Tang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Min Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (21665027); the YMU-DEAKIN International Associated Laboratory on Functional Materials, Education Department of Yunnan Province (117–02001001002107); the Foundation of Education Bureau of Yunnan Province (2020J0327) and the Science and Technology Department of Yunnan Province (202105AC160055).
